So like, see, Carla helps a lot because I can hand stuff off and know that it's not going to get very far away from where it needs to be. So like the books, see before the book goes to the publisher it goes from me to her. And so she'll format it and do chapter breaks and create little graphic boxes and that kind of bullshit to tell them what to do. And I'll look at it before it goes and maybe I'll tweak something, maybe I won't, but there's a whole little pile of stuff I didn't have to do. Well, she's been doing it for 23 years, so, you know, there's speed there. But the big thing is you just got to decide you're going to do it. And ideally you're going to do it every day or four days a week and one not or one day a week, the whole day, every Friday. But sort of the consistency of it is what gets you to subconscious efficacy, not just conscious efficacy. And see, like all the checklists and stuff, see, I rarely miss without looking at them. I rarely, I don't like right to fill, but when I do a newsletter I'm usually no more than half a page over or half a page under when I first hand it to her. I didn't count words, I didn't count pages, I didn't do anything. It's just in my head now how much stuff is needed in order to fill that space. Well, the only way you get that is, you know, doing it. And it's a big, it's like for me in racing, it's like reps, you know. I mean, if I'm only driving three, four times a week and I'm competing against a guy who drove 212 times this week, he'll be better than I am. I mean, it's just that simple, because he's doing stuff unconsciously that I'm having to do consciously. And that slows you down enormously. So you kind of got to decide, and you sort of, obviously you sort of made those decisions by being here. You kind of got to decide that, okay, the business I'm in is really influencing people by words in print, online or offline. And therefore, I'm going to get really good at it. And I'm going to kind of define myself in that way. Does that help you? Okay. Anybody else from, yep. Do you have any creativity all the way, or more of it is that pretty much all you do is you take something that's over here and then just redesign it in a way that's... As much as I can. Yeah. If by creativity you mean like blank slate creativity, that's the enemy of speed. And the true fiction guy who's not writing serial fiction, so like Stephen King. See, that's what he does. So therefore it takes him a lot of time to do a book because he's starting with a blank slate. I don't know who the characters are. I don't know what the story's going to be. I don't know where the story's going to go. I don't know where the story's going to end up. Well, that's a mess. Now, I'm never going to be a great American novelist because I'm not doing that. But yes, so now if by creativity you sort of mean stylistic issues, then again, I don't edit much anymore, but 10 years ago I used to edit quite a bit. So I go back and gee, can this phrase be said more cleverly? Should there be a different analogy used? Now again, I'm like two drafts, but that's because it's... See, I don't have to like the exercise of 22 different ways to say thin in the checklist. I gave you for that. And are we going to say this time? Are we going to say serious is cancer? Are we going to say serious is the chili at a truck stop in Laredo? Or are we going to say, see, I don't do that by manually making that list and picking one. Whereas if you're not doing it a lot, that's what you got to do. And it's important whether you say serious is cancer or serious is mom's chili at a truck stop at Laredo. You're going for a different reaction to that depending on which you say. Now, I get it right or the way I want it anyway, pretty much automatically. So maybe on a page, I'm agonizing over one of those. Ten years ago, I would agonized over 20. It slows you down. Now, I also have some lists that are pre-done. So for example, the serious is cancer list I actually have. So if I need it, I got a list with 100 I'm already done. I can go take a look at. I've accumulated over years. But I did build a lot of it early. In direct response copywriting, when I first started to read Halbert's stuff, Halbert said, here's how you get good. Number one, you go re-copy ads by hand because that ingrains it in the subconscious just exactly the way they're written. You take file cards and you dissect all the ads into pieces and put the stuff on file cards. And finally, you get around to rewriting the ad and trying to make it better. Well, I actually did that for like a year. I mean, I'll bet you there aren't 10 people in the country who've done it. So I got actually like 600 file cards with a paragraph on this, a starter phrase on that, and that's all from my first year. And I haven't done it since. But I actually went and did the work. And I'll bet you there aren't 10 people in the country who've actually done that. They may have done it once, but I'll bet they haven't done like 100 ads. But he's right. See, it sort of trains the subconscious because direct response copy is about rhythm, not just about words. And the rhythm can't be mechanically, you know, like they say, you either got rhythm or you don't, right? So, and the way you get it is by creating it through reps of taking ads that have good rhythm and writing them. And now your subconscious gets rhythm. Well, you don't use bullets. Let me just do a check for one second. I'm sorry, Charlie, but just out of curiosity, what did you mean by creativity? Fat or like slate? I'm going to write a book. I need to come up with this new unique concept that no one else has thought about or look at what's been written before and then tailor it to my situation so it's not plagiarism, obviously. Theft stopping short of plagiarism is a lot faster. Sorry, Charlie. I'm sorry, Charlie. I was wondering about how you learned and then take out observation. The thirty-one principles. The question, and your checklist. You pull out, think you're rich, and say, hey, why is this book endured? And their four stories were, can you talk a little about this tonight? You want to be a more humorous speaker? What is Lennon doing? Well, yeah, like take that example. Okay, so when I started speaking, like most beginning speakers, I told jokes to try and be funny. So you took a canned joke out of a joke book, you know, so like, you know, Cavitz advised everybody is, tell the pony story. You can't go wrong with the pony story. And by the way, you can't. People like the damn thing, even if they've heard it five hundred times, but you know, I hated the damn thing from the beginning, but I used it for a year because I didn't have anything better, right? And then you gradually move from canned jokes to learning how to make the joke your own, and then ultimately to tell stories that can be uniquely yours. And you know, you're not doing comedy club stuff, so it doesn't have to be. But the only way to learn timing, which is 80% of being funny on the stage with a story is not what you're telling, but how you tell it. Now, like Chris and those guys, so I told them the teddy bear story last night, you know, at the dinner table. Now, that's a really good story, but you can tell it and not get any laughs. I mean, it's got a, and so like if I get a new one, I tell it, if I'm going to think about using it on the stage, I tell it to the people at the track, I tell it to Carl, I tell it to Miltier, 8, 10, 12 times to experiment with, gee, is it funny or if he's, if it's a blue shirt instead of a red shirt, is it funny? And so, so when I first started to try and figure this out, right, first of all, I went and got a bunch of reference tools. So there's reference tools for everything. So like story structure, like you asked me about, see how to write fiction, I got 400 books on that. Plus some, I haven't kept the books, but I kept two pages out of a book, you know. So there's reference stuff. There's actually teaching on how to tell humorous stories. In NSA world, the best guys got my name at Charlie Jarvis, and Charlie has, like his bird story. If you learn the bird story with his timing, you learn timing. So Carson for timing. By the way, if you watch Bill Maher, if you watch Bill Maher, go get a DVD of Carson and watch a Carson monologue, and then immediately watch a Bill Maher monologue. The physical mannerisms are the same. I mean, it's really incredible. I mean, Maher had to have like internalized and now channel Johnny's physicality. But yeah, the answer to this is it actually takes some work, you know, to get good at it. So if you decide you're going to be a good direct response copywriter, there's things you've got to do to get past cut and paste. You can get so far with cut and paste. So you can take my copywriting stuff, Bill's copywriting stuff, and somebody in many cases they can make money, you know, but they're never going to be, you know, nobody's ever going to give him $100,000 to write a sales letter, and I mean, you know, and the same thing with this kind of writing. And you've got to study fiction, not just nonfiction, because again, my point is in reality, we're doing fiction. We're telling stories, we're creating compelling characters. We're, you know, like Charlie said, it's easier if you're an interesting person with a lot of interesting life experiences. Well, actually, most people have them. They just don't like know they got them and they don't know how to turn them into...
